trans-unit.source: Graduated classification
trans-unit.target: Graduated classification

trans-unit.source: __🔙[Back to Homepage](/content/intro.md)__
trans-unit.target: __🔙[Back to Homepage](/content/intro.md)__

trans-unit.source: Graduated classification in GIS involves categorizing spatial data into **classes or ranges** based on a progression of values.
trans-unit.target: Graduated classification in GIS involves categorizing spatial data into **classes or ranges** based on a progression of values.

trans-unit.source: This method is particularly useful for visualizing quantitative data, allowing for the differentiation of intensity, density, or magnitude across a spectrum, facilitating a nuanced representation of geographic phenomena.
trans-unit.target: This method is particularly useful for visualizing quantitative data, allowing for the differentiation of intensity, density, or magnitude across a spectrum, facilitating a nuanced representation of geographic phenomena.

trans-unit.source: Graduated classification is used for quantitative data, usually __interval__ or __ratio__ scaled.
trans-unit.target: Graduated classification is used for quantitative data, usually __interval__ or __ratio__ scaled.

trans-unit.source: Data Scale
trans-unit.target: Data Scale

trans-unit.source: Definition
trans-unit.target: Definition

trans-unit.source: Example
trans-unit.target: Example

trans-unit.source: Typical Data Format
trans-unit.target: Typical Data Format

trans-unit.source: Interval Scale
trans-unit.target: Interval Scale

trans-unit.source: Equal intervals between values, no true zero point
trans-unit.target: Equal intervals between values, no true zero point

trans-unit.source: Temperature (Celsius)
trans-unit.target: Temperature (Celsius)

trans-unit.source: Float (44.5 Degree)
trans-unit.target: Float (44.5 Degree)

trans-unit.source: Ratio Scale
trans-unit.target: Ratio Scale

trans-unit.source: Equal intervals with a true zero point
trans-unit.target: Equal intervals with a true zero point

trans-unit.source: Population, Length, Number of trees
trans-unit.target: Population, Length, Number of trees

trans-unit.source: Integer (5 Trees) or Float (12.5 km of Road)
trans-unit.target: Integer (5 Trees) or Float (12.5 km of Road)

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Video: Applying a graduated classification to a layer
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Video: Applying a graduated classification to a layer
:::

trans-unit.source: __To classify data in classes…__
trans-unit.target: __To classify data in classes…__

trans-unit.source: Right-click on your layer
trans-unit.target: Right-click on your layer

trans-unit.source: Click on `Symbology`
trans-unit.target: Click on `Symbology`

trans-unit.source: Click on `Graduated`
trans-unit.target: Click on `Graduated`

trans-unit.source: In the `Value` dropdown menu select the column based on which you want to classify your data.
trans-unit.target: In the `Value` dropdown menu select the column based on which you want to classify your data.

trans-unit.source: Downright select the number of classes you want to use.
trans-unit.target: Downright select the number of classes you want to use.

trans-unit.source: Under `Mode` select the classification method you want to use e.g. Equal count (Quantile).
trans-unit.target: Under `Mode` select the classification method you want to use e.g. Equal count (Quantile).

trans-unit.source: Click on `Classify`. Now you should see all classes and the distribution of values. To add or delete single classes use the `-` and `+` buttons.
trans-unit.target: Click on `Classify`. Now you should see all classes and the distribution of values. To add or delete single classes use the `-` and `+` buttons.

trans-unit.source: *Optional*: Click on `Histogram` → `Load Values`. Now you can see the exact distribution of values over the classes. This is very practical to decide on a classification method. You can also check the mean value and standard deviation. :::{figure} /fig/Graduated_histogram.png
trans-unit.target: *Optional*: Click on `Histogram` → `Load Values`. Now you can see the exact distribution of values over the classes. This is very practical to decide on a classification method. You can also check the mean value and standard deviation. :::{figure} /fig/Graduated_histogram.png

trans-unit.source: *Optional*: In the `Symbol` dropdown menu you can select the colours and symbols you want to use.
trans-unit.target: *Optional*: In the `Symbol` dropdown menu you can select the colours and symbols you want to use.

trans-unit.source: *Optional*: In the `Color ramp` dropdown menu you can specify the range of colours you want to use. To see all color ramps click on the down arrow of the `Color ramp` → `All Color Ramps`.
trans-unit.target: *Optional*: In the `Color ramp` dropdown menu you can specify the range of colours you want to use. To see all color ramps click on the down arrow of the `Color ramp` → `All Color Ramps`.

trans-unit.source: *Optional*: Under `Legend Format` you can adjust how precise the range of the classes will be displayed in the legend. Usually, it is practical to not use too complicated numbers in the legend.
trans-unit.target: *Optional*: Under `Legend Format` you can adjust how precise the range of the classes will be displayed in the legend. Usually, it is practical to not use too complicated numbers in the legend.

trans-unit.source: *Optional*: You can open the panel `Layer Rendering` on the button of the window. Here you can adjust the opacity/ transparency of the layer.
trans-unit.target: *Optional*: You can open the panel `Layer Rendering` on the button of the window. Here you can adjust the opacity/ transparency of the layer.

trans-unit.source: Click `Apply` to put your adjustment into effect.
trans-unit.target: Click `Apply` to put your adjustment into effect.

trans-unit.source: Click `OK` to close the window.
trans-unit.target: Click `OK` to close the window.

trans-unit.source: The number of classes
trans-unit.target: The number of classes

trans-unit.source: Deciding on the number of classes, and where the ranges for the different classes lie, has a profound impact on the resulting map.
trans-unit.target: Deciding on the number of classes, and where the ranges for the different classes lie, has a profound impact on the resulting map.

trans-unit.source: There are seven ways in QGIS to split quantitative data into classes.
trans-unit.target: There are seven ways in QGIS to split quantitative data into classes.

trans-unit.source: The four most important ones are: Equal intervals, Quantile, Natural breaks, Manual.
trans-unit.target: The four most important ones are: Equal intervals, Quantile, Natural breaks, Manual.

trans-unit.source: In general, you should limit the number of classes between 3 to 9.
trans-unit.target: In general, you should limit the number of classes between 3 to 9.

trans-unit.source: The impact of different class breaks on maps (Source: HeiGIT, adapted from [Axis Maps](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/data-classification)). :::
trans-unit.target: The impact of different class breaks on maps (Source: HeiGIT, adapted from [Axis Maps](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/data-classification)). :::
